refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall achieve end-to-end teleoperation communication latency with p95 ≤ 150 ms under agreed network conditions to enable safe and effective remote equipment operation.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-008 addresses a critical safety and operational requirement. Teleoperation latency directly impacts operator situational awareness, control responsiveness, and safety margins. The p95 percentile metric ensures consistent performance under variable network conditions. Refinement clarifies that this is an end-to-end requirement spanning sensor acquisition, network transmission, and command execution.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Measure round-trip latency (sensor → network → operator display → command input → network → actuator response) under representative network conditions (agreed bandwidth, jitter, packet loss profiles). Collect statistical distribution and confirm p95 ≤ 150 ms across 1000+ samples per test scenario.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-008.1: Telemetry acquisition and transmission latency shall be ≤ 50 ms (p95)
FR-NGHE-008.2: Network transport latency shall be ≤ 75 ms (p95) under agreed conditions
FR-NGHE-008.3: Command reception and actuation latency shall be ≤ 50 ms (p95)
FR-NGHE-008.4: Latency monitoring and alerting shall detect and report exceedances in real-time

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement edge-local telemetry buffering with asynchronous network batching and operator-side predictive display rendering
DP-B: Deploy dedicated low-latency network path with QoS prioritization, hardware-accelerated codec, and synchronized time-stamped sensor fusion
DP-C: Hybrid approach combining local sensor processing with adaptive network compression, multi-path routing, and latency-aware command queuing
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-C: Hybrid approach combining local sensor processing with adaptive network compression, multi-path routing, and latency-aware command queuing. This DP balances robustness (multi-path fallback), efficiency (adaptive compression), and determinism (latency-aware queuing) to meet the p95 ≤ 150 ms requirement across variable network conditions.

potential_sources: Coupling between sensor acquisition timing and network batching window (mitigated by local timestamping)
Coupling between compression algorithm selection and network bandwidth availability (mitigated by adaptive switching)
Coupling between command queue depth and actuator response time (mitigated by priority-based dequeuing)
coupling_check.notes: DP-C exhibits loose coupling through adaptive mechanisms. Each sub-component (local processing, compression, routing, queuing) can be tuned independently within design constraints. No circular dependencies detected.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Network infrastructure supports multi-path routing and QoS tagging. Risk: Legacy networks may not; mitigation via fallback to single-path with best-effort QoS.
Assumption: Operator display hardware supports 60+ Hz refresh for predictive rendering. Risk: Older operator stations may not; mitigation via graceful degradation to non-predictive display.
Assumption: Sensor hardware provides sub-10 ms acquisition latency. Risk: Legacy sensors may exceed this; mitigation via sensor upgrade or latency compensation in DP.
Assumption: Agreed network conditions are stable and documented. Risk: Undocumented network variability could violate p95 target; mitigation via continuous latency monitoring and alerting.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-C to explicitly specify: (1) adaptive compression algorithm selection criteria (e.g., switch from H.264 to MJPEG if bandwidth < 5 Mbps), (2) multi-path routing failover logic (e.g., primary LTE + secondary WiFi), (3) command queue maximum depth and priority levels, (4) latency measurement points and telemetry export format for verification.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Encapsulates all teleoperation-specific logic: sensor telemetry acquisition and transmission, network transport orchestration (multi-path routing, QoS management, adaptive compression), command reception and queuing, and latency monitoring. Interfaces with Vehicle for sensor/actuator access and Network Link for transport.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module exports timestamped sensor data (video, IMU, GNSS, proximity) to operator display and safety services. Latency-tagged for monitoring.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module receives operator commands (movement, tool actuation, mode changes) from remote operator station. Commands are queued with priority and latency metadata.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module uses Network Link for bidirectional communication. Manages multi-path routing, QoS tagging, and adaptive compression to meet latency targets.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module reports latency exceedances and network degradation events to Safety Services for operator notification and potential command inhibition.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the system-level container. Teleoperation is a major subsystem but not the entire solution. High diffusion risk if latency logic scattered across Vehicle, Safety Services, and Teleoperation.

Vehicle module owns actuators and local sensors but should not own teleoperation orchestration logic. Latency-aware queuing and network management belong in Teleoperation, not Vehicle. High diffusion risk if latency logic embedded in vehicle firmware.

Teleoperation module is the natural home for all teleoperation-specific latency management: sensor buffering, network transport orchestration, command queuing, and latency monitoring. Minimal diffusion risk. Clear ownership boundary.
Safety Services owns latency monitoring and alerting (SafetyAlert interface) but should not own the latency optimization logic itself. Partial containment for monitoring/alerting only.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the optimal container for DP-C implementation. It owns the end-to-end latency optimization logic (sensor buffering, adaptive compression, multi-path routing, command queuing) and exports latency metrics via SafetyAlert to Safety Services for monitoring. Vehicle module provides sensor/actuator interfaces; Network Link provides transport; Safety Services consumes latency alerts.
containment_risks: Risk: Teleoperation module may become a monolith if all latency optimization logic is centralized without clear sub-component boundaries. Mitigation: Define internal sub-modules (SensorBuffer, NetworkOrchestrator, CommandQueue, LatencyMonitor) with explicit interfaces.

Risk: Tight coupling between Teleoperation and Network Link if network-specific logic (e.g., LTE vs. WiFi failover) is hardcoded. Mitigation: Abstract network transport via a strategy pattern; Network Link provides multi-path capability.

Risk: Latency monitoring logic in Teleoperation may conflict with Safety Services' latency alerting logic. Mitigation: Teleoperation measures and exports raw latency data; Safety Services interprets and alerts based on policy.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Clarify Teleoperation module's internal architecture: (1) Define sub-components (SensorBuffer, NetworkOrchestrator, CommandQueue, LatencyMonitor) with explicit interfaces. (2) Specify how Teleoperation interacts with Vehicle for sensor/actuator access (e.g., via polling vs. event-driven callbacks). (3) Define Network Link contract: what multi-path routing and QoS capabilities must Network Link provide? (4) Specify SafetyAlert payload format for latency exceedances (e.g., JSON with timestamp, p95 value, network path info).

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-C (hybrid approach with local processing, adaptive compression, multi-path routing, and latency-aware queuing) directly addresses the refined FR requirement for p95 ≤ 150 ms end-to-end latency. The DP is decomposable into sub-DPs for each latency component (acquisition, transport, actuation, monitoring). No circular dependencies or missing functional coverage detected.

diffusion_risks: Risk: Latency optimization logic could diffuse across Vehicle (sensor buffering), Network Link (compression/routing), and Safety Services (monitoring) if ownership boundaries are not enforced. Mitigation: Teleoperation module owns all latency optimization; other modules provide interfaces only.
Risk: Adaptive compression algorithm selection logic could be duplicated in Teleoperation and Network Link. Mitigation: Centralize in Teleoperation; Network Link executes compression directives.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module achieves 0.95 containment score. Clear ownership boundary with minimal diffusion risk. External interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, Network Link, SafetyAlert) are well-defined and non-overlapping.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification is feasible via end-to-end latency measurement under agreed network conditions. Teleoperation module can export timestamped telemetry and command metadata for latency calculation. SafetyAlert interface enables real-time monitoring during testing.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: Teleoperation owns latency optimization; Vehicle provides sensor/actuator interfaces; Network Link provides transport; Safety Services consumes latency alerts. No ambiguity in responsibility.

recommended_revisions: Refine DP-C to specify adaptive compression algorithm selection criteria and multi-path failover logic.
Define Teleoperation module's internal sub-component architecture (SensorBuffer, NetworkOrchestrator, CommandQueue, LatencyMonitor).
Specify Network Link contract: what multi-path routing and QoS capabilities must it provide?
Define SafetyAlert payload format for latency exceedances.
Document agreed network conditions (bandwidth, jitter, packet loss profiles) as baseline for p95 target.

decision.justification: The refined FR is decomposable into four atomic sub-FRs (telemetry latency, network transport latency, command actuation latency, latency monitoring). DP-C is sound and well-mapped to Teleoperation module with high containment (0.95) and low diffusion risk. However, the sub-FRs require further decomposition to define specific design parameters for each latency component (e.g., sensor buffering strategy, compression algorithm selection, multi-path routing logic, command queue management). Proceeding to next level will clarify implementation details and reduce design risk.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-008.1: Telemetry acquisition and transmission latency shall be ≤ 50 ms (p95)
FR-NGHE-008.2: Network transport latency shall be ≤ 75 ms (p95) under agreed conditions
FR-NGHE-008.3: Command reception and actuation latency shall be ≤ 50 ms (p95)
FR-NGHE-008.4: Latency monitoring and alerting shall detect and report exceedances in real-time

assumptions: Network infrastructure supports multi-path routing and QoS tagging.
Operator display hardware supports 60+ Hz refresh for predictive rendering.
Sensor hardware provides sub-10 ms acquisition latency.
Agreed network conditions are stable and documented.
Vehicle module provides sensor/actuator interfaces with known latency characteristics.
Safety Services can consume and act on latency alerts via SafetyAlert interface.
open_risks: Legacy network infrastructure may not support multi-path routing or QoS tagging.
Operator station hardware may not support predictive rendering.
Sensor hardware latency may exceed design assumptions.
Network conditions may vary beyond agreed baseline.
Latency monitoring overhead could impact overall system latency.
Adaptive compression algorithm selection logic could introduce unpredictable latency spikes.
evidence_needed: Network infrastructure capability assessment (multi-path routing, QoS support).
Operator station hardware specifications (display refresh rate, processing capability).
Sensor hardware latency characterization (acquisition time, output rate).
Agreed network conditions documentation (bandwidth, jitter, packet loss profiles).
Latency measurement methodology and tools (e.g., timestamped telemetry, network packet capture).
Prototype or simulation results demonstrating p95 ≤ 150 ms under agreed conditions.
